---
title: "Discipline"
---

The directives in the SKILL are not style. Each one closes a specific way the
algebra breaks. Here they are stated as laws with the failure they prevent.

## Smallest action first

::: {.axiom title="Use the smallest action that falsifies the assumption"}
Before building anything, find the one-line `exec` or `console` read that proves
or disproves what you believe. Inspection is cheap; rework is not.
:::

Before writing mesh or material code, ask whether Unity has a one-liner. It
almost always does.

```{.exec name="A primitive cube, the whole way"}
var go = UnityEngine.GameObject.CreatePrimitive(UnityEngine.PrimitiveType.Cube);
go.name = "Cube";
go.transform.position = UnityEngine.Vector3.zero;
return "created: " + go.name;
```

`CreatePrimitive` already attaches a MeshFilter, a MeshRenderer with a default
material, and a collider. Hand-building that geometry is a larger, more
fallible mutation that earns nothing.

## No speculative APIs

::: {.failure title="Two failed guesses means stop guessing"}
If a guessed type or method fails once, probe the assembly. If a
material or mesh API fails twice on an unknown surface, stop and look up the
correct API from first principles. A third guess is how corruption happens.
:::

The dead-API table in the [Reference](../reference/dead-apis.qmd) exists because
these guesses were made and recorded so they need never be made again.

## One minimal fix per cycle

A cycle is: apply one fix, compile, verify. Stacking two fixes before a compile
means a red console cannot tell you which fix was wrong. The bracket law applies
to your edits as much as to the editor's state.

```{.cli name="One cycle"}
unity-cli editor refresh --compile
unity-cli console --type error
```

If the console is red, you changed exactly one thing and you know which.

## Shell quoting is part of the contract

Any non-trivial C# — quotes, interpolation, generics, lambdas, LINQ, multiple
statements — goes through stdin with a named heredoc, never inline. Inline
quoting produces fake failures that look like Unity errors but are really shell
errors.

```{.wrong lang="bash"}
unity-cli exec "var c = Camera.main; if (c != null) { return 'done'; }"
```

```{.right lang="bash"}
echo 'var c = Camera.main; return c != null ? "found" : "none";' | unity-cli exec
```

The single quotes in the wrong form are not valid C# string delimiters, and the
shell mangles the rest. The right form uses stdin so the C# is C# and the shell
stays out of it.

## Success must be measured

::: {.axiom title="Done means a green check, named and shown"}
A task is done only when its effect is directly verified: the object exists at
the path, the compile succeeded, the entity query returns the expected count.
Never claim done while a critical check is red, and never hide a red check
behind a vaguer green one.
:::

The [Reporting](../primitives/verify.qmd) discipline gives the shape: what was
inspected, what was changed, the compile result, the verification result, and
the remaining blocker if any — in that order, never compressed into a vague
success.
